title: Tea loaf
subtitle: Dried fruit soaked overnight in tea, no added fat
excerpt: Mixed dried fruit soaked overnight in strong black tea, then baked with egg and flour into a dense, moist loaf. No butter in the batter: the tea-soaked fruit provides all the moisture.
sourceNotes: Tea loaf appears in British household baking guides from the Victorian period, using the technique of soaking dried fruit in tea to both plump the fruit and add flavour without relying on fat for moisture. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, records similar fruit loaf methods. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), documents regional British tea breads. The overnight soak is central to the recipe.

content.text: Tea loaf is not a loaf in the bread sense; it is a fruit cake baked in a loaf tin, dense and close-crumbed, made without butter or oil. The moisture comes entirely from the fruit and the residual tea. It requires the
; a two-hour soak produces a noticeably inferior result. Serve in thin slices with a little butter.

Put the dried fruit and sugar in a large bowl. Pour the hot tea over them, stir to dissolve the sugar, and leave to soak overnight or for at least 8 hours. By morning, most of the liquid will have been absorbed into the fruit.

Preheat the oven to 160°C conventional. Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line with baking paper. Stir the flour, mixed spice, and beaten egg into the soaked fruit. Mix until evenly combined; the batter will be stiff. Spoon into the tin and level the surface. Bake for 70 to 80 minutes until a skewer comes out clean, the top is dark brown and set, and the loaf pulls slightly from the tin sides. If the top darkens before the centre is through, lay a loose sheet of foil over it after 50 minutes. Leave in the tin for 15 minutes before turning out. The loaf is best kept wrapped for a day before cutting.
